old_claim.note: Germ Theory of Disease provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because early slow sand filtration was deployed empirically before germ theory became the explanatory framework for later water disinfection and sanitary science.

source_shape.source_locator: Historical review chronology of water treatment, including early sand filtration before later bacteriological understanding.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The review describes water-treatment chronology with filtration practice predating modern germ-theory-driven disinfection.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the scoped slow-sand node is anchored to early filtration practice, germ theory can explain and improve later public-health treatment without being a prerequisite for the original filtration method.

ontology_before: The graph projected later microbiological explanation onto an earlier empirical filtration process.
ontology_after: Slow sand filtration keeps earlier filtration lineage; germ theory belongs on later disinfection, bacteriology, and sanitary public-health nodes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-10-random-sample-quality-fixes keeps this direct edge absent.
would_reject_if: The slow_sand_filtration node were narrowed to bacteriologically managed slow sand filters only.
A source showed the first scoped slow sand filtration deployment required germ theory.
A later graph split created a separate modern biologically managed slow-sand-filtration node.
short_reason: Slow sand filtration predates germ theory as a required explanatory framework.
